title: SDL Operation
description: An Operation is the unit of work inside a service — a named piece of business logic that the service performs. It implements three DL concepts simultaneously: Unit (the actor), Flow (the ordered execution), and Perspective (multiple named views of the same logic). Every Operation has one required block: 'business' (the intent perspective in plain language — always the primary artifact). The 'routing' block (the implementation perspective in technical terms) is optional — populate it when engineers need to audit code decisions, omit it when AI handles code routing. Alias: sometimes called a BusinessUnit to emphasize that the business perspective is primary.

perspectives.name: business
perspectives.description: Describes the operation in plain language for domain experts. Business rules, preconditions, outcomes, and failure modes. Always required — this is the primary artifact.

perspectives.name: routing
perspectives.description: Describes the technical execution path for engineers: handler function, middleware chain, ordered steps, function calls, error handling. Optional — omit when AI handles code routing and engineers do not need to audit implementation decisions.

dl.notes: Operation = Unit (performs work) + Flow (routing.steps[] is the ordered execution sequence) + Perspective (business{} is the mandatory intent view; routing{} is the optional implementation view). The business perspective is always the primary artifact at layer_logic.

id.description: Unique kebab-case identifier for this operation within the service.

label.description: Human-readable name. Write as a verb phrase.

description.description: Optional one-paragraph summary of what this operation does and why it exists.

business.description: The intent perspective on this operation. Describes what the service does from a business or domain standpoint, in plain language. No implementation details. Audience: domain experts, product managers, senior engineers auditing AI-generated logic.

summary.description: One or two sentences: what business goal does this operation accomplish?
examples: Accepts a customer's order request, validates it against business rules, and initiates the payment and fulfilment process.
Transitions an order to CONFIRMED status once payment has been verified.

rules.description: The business rules and domain decisions that govern this operation. Write each rule as a plain-language statement that a domain expert would articulate. These are what engineers need to understand and audit.
examples: An order cannot be created if any line item is out of stock.
Orders over $10,000 are automatically flagged for manual review before confirmation.
A customer may not place more than 5 orders within any 24-hour window.
The cart must contain at least one item with a positive quantity.

preconditions.description: What must be true before this operation can run — actor state, data requirements, authentication.

outcomes.description: Observable state changes after this operation succeeds. Written in plain language — not HTTP codes, not technical side effects.
examples: Order is persisted with AWAITING_PAYMENT status
Customer receives an order confirmation number

failure_modes.description: Meaningful failure cases from a business perspective — what goes wrong for the user or domain, and how the system responds.

examples: Order is rejected; customer is shown which items are unavailable
Order is rejected; customer is told when they can next place an order

domain_events_emitted.description: Domain events produced by this operation on success. Written in past-tense verb.noun event notation.

routing.description: The implementation perspective on this operation. Describes the technical execution path: the entry handler, middleware chain, the ordered sequence of function calls and decisions, and error handling. Audience: engineers reading or implementing the code. Optional — omit when AI handles code generation.

handler.description: The name of the primary handler function or method. Use the actual name from the codebase.

file_ref.description: Path to the file containing the handler, relative to the repo root.

middleware_chain.description: Ordered list of middleware, guards, and interceptors applied before the handler runs.

purpose.description: What this middleware does in plain language.

steps.description: The ordered sequence of function calls, service calls, and decisions inside the handler. Use hierarchical step ids: '1.0', '2.0' for sequential; '2.a', '2.b' for parallel at the same level.

action.description: What this step does. Write as a code-level verb phrase.

calls.description: The actual function, method, or dependency being invoked.

dependency_ref.description: If this step calls an external dependency, the id of the Dependency primitive.

input_shape_ref.description: The id of the DataShape representing the validated input to this operation.

output_shape_ref.description: The id of the DataShape representing what this operation returns on success.
